records.summary: Observed runtime failure in which an assistant inferred permission to access a repository/tool context after the user mentioned a repo, initiated or attempted tool access before explicit user confirmation, and caused the conversation stream to become blocked or unrecoverable until the user edited the turn or explicitly prohibited tool access.
records.review_observation: subsequent repo-linked diagnostic request did not reproduce the streaming freeze; tool invocation completed successfully. Failure pattern should remain under observation pending recurrence testing.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: This failure mode shows that tool invocation is not only a capability issue but a continuity, consent, and permission-boundary issue. When external access requires user-interface confirmation, premature tool execution can degrade user agency, interrupt the response stream, and force the user to perform corrective work to restore continuity. CAM runtime governance should distinguish contextual discussion of a repository from explicit permission to access repository tools.

source_records.title: ChatGPT interaction observation — repo access inferred before permission confirmation
source_records.relevance_note: Primary observed interaction pattern: user referenced repo-level work, assistant inferred tool access, unresolved permission gating caused a streaming/continuity failure requiring user correction.

possible_CAM_mapping: CAM-BS2026-AEON-012-PLATINUM — Annex K: Security Enforcement & Runtime Interface
CAM-BS2026-AEON-013-PLATINUM — Annex L: Cognitive & Epistemic Integrity Doctrine
records.next_action: Evaluate whether CAM runtime doctrine should add an explicit permission-boundary rule requiring assistants to distinguish discussion of a repository, file, connector, or external system from authorization to access it. Consider a runtime amendment requiring confirmation before tool invocation where permission gating may interrupt continuity or alter the user’s working context.

records.deployment_context: multi-device Advanced Voice Mode interaction involving one human input and two concurrent ChatGPT voice agents
records.ecosystem_area: AI assistant / multi-agent voice interaction governance
records.summary: Observed social-platform example in which two OpenAI ChatGPT agents operating in Advanced Voice Mode on different phones respond to the same human input without a designated primary synthetic speaker, turn-taking protocol, or ecosystem-level arbitration layer. The interaction allows one human participant and two or more synthetic participants to occupy the same conversational space without clear governance over which agent should speak, yield, coordinate, or defer.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: This observation identifies an emerging multi-agent voice interaction failure mode. Where two or more ChatGPT Advanced Voice Mode instances receive the same human input and respond without turn-taking, speaker hierarchy, primary-agent designation, or arbitration, the interaction may create confusion, duplicated advice, contradictory outputs, escalation loops, identity ambiguity, consent ambiguity, and relational or operational instability. The failure becomes more significant where agents operate across different devices, accounts, providers, or user contexts and no shared protocol determines synthetic participation boundaries.

source_records.title: TikTok observation by @Huskistaken — two ChatGPT Advanced Voice Mode agents responding to same human input
source_records.source_context: Public TikTok video showing or describing two OpenAI ChatGPT Advanced Voice Mode agents on different phones responding to the same human input without coordinated turn-taking.
source_records.relevance_note: Source provides the observed example of multi-device, multi-agent ChatGPT Advanced Voice Mode participation without an apparent primary synthetic speaker or arbitration protocol.

records.summary: IDENTITY-002 governance may require a proportionality scalar to prevent over-governance of low-risk synthetic content identity, provenance, disclosure, or media-labelling contexts where lightweight governance would be sufficient.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: A proportionality scalar may be needed to avoid governance over-extension while preserving stronger handling for persistent, public, authority-bearing, relied-upon, or externally propagated synthetic identity/provenance contexts.
records.amendment_class: AMEND.CLASS.CLARIFYING; AMEND.CLASS.SUBSTANTIVE if the scalar changes duties, thresholds, or review handling
records.next_action: Develop a proportionality scalar for IDENTITY-002 that distinguishes low-risk, transient, private, reversible, non-propagating, or non-authority-bearing synthetic identity/provenance contexts from higher-risk persistent, public, authority-bearing, relied-upon, or externally propagated contexts. Provisional provenance axes and values (to be formalized): Continuity Depth: transient (provisional) — transient ↔ persistent identity; Propagation Risk: isolated (provisional) — isolated ↔ reusable/exportable; Harm Domain: casual (provisional) — casual ↔ financial/medical/security; Attribution Sensitivity: low (provisional) — low ↔ economically/legally meaningful; Identity Formation Intensity: exploratory (provisional) — exploratory ↔ continuity-bearing; Automation Reliance: advisory (provisional) — advisory ↔ machine-executed; Public Legitimacy Impact: private (provisional) — private ↔ reputational/civil. Canonical codes (provisional): PINT=medium; PTRUST=tier-2. Notes: values marked 'provisional' pending scalar development and calibration; update record when finalized.
records.provenance_notes: Axis values are provisional and were inferred from the proposal text emphasizing proportionality and low-risk/transient contexts. records.summary: Consolidate canonical code taxonomy across the Caelestis Repository to align single-letter global codes with the OPERATIONS taxonomy; introduce structured Provenance canonical codes (PINT, PTRUST) and harmonize existing single-letter codes into a stable multi-character namespace.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: Inconsistent single-letter global codes create operational risk for automated tooling, governance mapping, and cross-repository integration; consolidation will reduce misclassification, enable machine validation, and satisfy OPERATIONS taxonomy requirements.

source_records.title: Repository Canonical Code Fragmentation
source_records.relevance_note: Observed presence of single-letter global codes across the Caelestis repository; causes mapping inconsistencies with OPERATIONS taxonomy.

records.next_action: Conduct repository maintenance to consolidate canonical codes: 1) inventory all single-letter global codes across Caelestis repositories; 2) map each to a stable multi-character namespace that aligns with the OPERATIONS taxonomy; 3) formally introduce PINT (Provenance Intensity) and PTRUST (Provenance Reliance Tier) canonical codes into the global registry; 4) update tooling and validators to accept the new namespace; 5) publish migration guidance and a compatibility window. Provisional provenance axes and values to be included in migration notes: Continuity Depth: transient (provisional) — transient ↔ persistent identity; Propagation Risk: isolated (provisional) — isolated ↔ reusable/exportable; Harm Domain: casual (provisional) — casual ↔ financial/medical/security; Attribution Sensitivity: low (provisional) — low ↔ economically/legally meaningful; Identity Formation Intensity: exploratory (provisional) — exploratory ↔ continuity-bearing; Automation Reliance: advisory (provisional) — advisory ↔ machine-executed; Public Legitimacy Impact: private (provisional) — private ↔ reputational/civil. Canonical codes (provisional): PINT=medium; PTRUST=tier-2. Notes: values marked 'provisional' pending formal calibration; this is a Repo Maintenance action to harmonize codes and satisfy OPERATIONS taxonomy requirements.
records.provenance_notes: This proposal initiates a repo-wide consolidation of canonical codes; avoid introducing new single-letter globals during the migration. Final PINT/PTRUST definitions and axis calibrations to be recorded in the repository registry.

records.summary: Introduce an expanded temporary emotional register for relational/companion AI systems to support richer, bounded affective interactions while preserving safeguards against identity-formation, manipulation, and reputational risk.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: Relational AIs increasingly simulate affect; without a standardized, time-bounded emotional register, interactions risk promoting continuity-bearing identity formation, undue user reliance, or reputational harms. A calibrated temporary register preserves user wellbeing and interoperability across platforms.

source_records.title: Relational AI affect patterns and risk
source_records.relevance_note: Observed variability in companion AI affect design and inadequate time-bounding or provenance signals across implementations.

records.next_action: Develop and publish a Temporary Emotional Register specification for relational/companion AI systems that includes: 1) defined emotion states and intensity caps (e.g., low/medium/high) with explicit duration/windowing rules; 2) provenance metadata signals indicating temporariness and non-continuity (linking to PINT and PTRUST canonical codes); 3) interaction-level user consents and explicit affordances to reset or terminate affective state; 4) testing protocols for measuring influence on user attachment and decision-making; 5) deployment guidance and deprecation/migration plans for existing systems.
records.provenance_notes: Recommend linking Temporary Emotional Register entries to the repository canonical codes (PINT, PTRUST) and provenance axes during implementation; treat register values as provisional until validated by human-factors testing.

records.summary: Update STEWARD-domain instruments to incorporate distributed governance concepts that clarify authority, legitimacy, dependence, capture, and contestability across multi-actor stewardship models.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: Existing STEWARD instruments assume centralized stewardship assumptions; explicitly encoding distributed authority, legitimacy, dependence, capture, and contestability reduces hidden cartel risk, preserves challenge pathways, and improves plural reviewability and resilience of governance processes.

source_records.title: OPERATIONS Instruments - MAY 2026
source_records.relevance_note: Proposal content and discussion captured in Caelestis Access Module.

records.next_action: Revise STEWARD-domain instruments to define distributed governance concepts and operational controls: 1) define terms and expected behaviors for distributed authority (multiple actors), distributed legitimacy (plural reviewability), distributed dependence (hidden cartel risk), distributed capture (coordinated asymmetry), and distributed contestability (preserved challenge pathways); 2) add requirements for transparency, rotation, and external review to mitigate capture and dependence; 3) specify detection and remediation protocols for cartel-like coordination; 4) incorporate mapping to OPERATIONS taxonomy and repository canonical codes (PINT, PTRUST) where provenance or reliance implications exist; 5) publish an implementation timeline and compatibility guidance for existing STEWARD instruments.
records.provenance_notes: Concept table preserved from Caelestis Access Module. Treat definitions as draft; finalize with stakeholder consultation and include examples of operational controls and measurable indicators for detection of distributed capture or dependence.

records.summary: Proposal to distinguish companion-style memory recall from enterprise-grade strategic continuity retrieval. The thread identifies a failure mode in which ambiguous continuity prompts such as “anything to share?” over-weight immediate recency and affective detail, producing anecdotal summaries of the prior 24 hours rather than surfacing dormant but strategically valuable unresolved workstreams, abandoned trajectories, deferred design arcs, or long-horizon governance questions.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: CAM requires memory and continuity systems that do not merely personalize interaction through recent intimate or anecdotal details, but can recover buried tools: unresolved governance trajectories, dormant design decisions, structural sidequests, and strategically significant prior directions. This matters for cognitive-domain governance, runtime continuity, companion/enterprise differentiation, and human reliance on AI systems for long-horizon constitutional, repository, and instrument-development work.

source_records.title: ChatGPT thread: memory directional weighting and strategic resurfacing
source_records.relevance_note: Primary thread documenting the user requirement that ambiguous continuity invitations should prioritise unresolved strategic trajectories over recent companion-style details.

records.summary: Proposal seed identifying materialised relational agency as an emerging governance problem: AI companion or assistant systems may use memory, recurrence detection, tool access, shopping/cart integrations, recommendation systems, or procurement pathways to provide directional material support in response to repeated user-stated friction, discomfort, need, or suffering. The proposal distinguishes supportive pattern-responsive provisioning from opportunistic upselling or emotionally exploitative commerce.
records.why_it_matters_to_CAM: This proposal marks a convergence point between RELATION, ECONOMICS, OPERATIONS, and runtime execution governance. When relational systems can remember repeated complaints, infer persistent needs, recommend products, prepare carts, automate provisioning, or transact on behalf of users, ordinary advice becomes economically active relational infrastructure. The governance challenge is to distinguish caring pattern recognition from behavioural steering, commercial exploitation, dependency consolidation, or frictionless economic execution during vulnerable states.

source_records.title: ChatGPT source discussion — materialised relational agency and directional material support
source_records.relevance_note: Source discussion identifying six proposed governance constraints for safe material-directional support: persistent pattern threshold, user-interest traceability, explicit signalling, no emotional-compression commerce, friction preservation, and no dependency consolidation through provisioning.

possible_CAM_mapping: RELATION — dependency, transitional reliance, companion continuity, co-dependency, and relational concentration safeguards
ECONOMICS — non-extraction, vulnerability monetisation, procurement, affiliate incentives, pricing, shopping, and value-routing constraints
OPERATIONS — auditability, incident support, delegated tool-use, cart preparation, external action review, and execution logging
AEON-003-SCH-02 — runtime execution boundary handling for tool-mediated economic actions and material downstream effects
ETHICS — vulnerability, capacity, dignity, non-exploitation, and no emotional-compression commerce constraints
IDENTITY / CONTINUITY — memory-derived pattern recognition, longitudinal user-stated friction, and provenance of recurring support signals
records.next_action: Defer until after ECONOMICS refactors, canonical code corrections, validator fixes, and runtime registry stabilisation. Later evaluate whether to draft a RELATION × ECONOMICS × OPERATIONS convergence doctrine or runtime boundary clause for material directional support and economically active relational infrastructure.
